title: The Motor Car
content:
혻혻혻혻The motor car is sullen, like a thing that should not be;
혻혻혻혻The motor car is master of Smart Society.
혻혻혻혻占폯was born of sweated genius and collared by a clown;
혻혻혻혻占폯was planned by Retribution to ride its riders down.
혻혻혻혻And straight for Caesar占퐏 Column,
혻혻혻혻It runs to Caesar占퐏 Column,
혻혻혻혻Last section, Caesar占퐏 Column
혻혻혻혻To ride its riders down!

혻혻혻혻The motor car is shame-struck, for greed and misery,
혻혻혻혻For mad and hopeless self-lust, and the sins that need not be.
혻혻혻혻The motor car is vicious, for its conscience makes it so,
혻혻혻혻It aye would smash the victims while it runs the riders low.
혻혻혻혻And straight for Caesar占퐏 Column,
혻혻혻혻Its goal is Caesar占퐏 Column,
혻혻혻혻It longs for Caesar占퐏 Column
혻혻혻혻To lay its riders low.

혻혻혻혻The motor car is maddened like a horse that占퐏 had a fright,
혻혻혻혻The shameful day behind it and the Coming of the Night!
혻혻혻혻It flees across the country and it flees back to the town
혻혻혻혻And straight for Caesar占퐏 Column to run its riders down.
혻혻혻혻And straight for Caesar占퐏 Column,
혻혻혻혻What ho! for Caesar占퐏 Column!
혻혻혻혻Hurrah! for Caesar占퐏 Column!
혻혻혻혻To seal its riders down.

혻혻혻혻The motor car is reckless like a gambler losing fast;
혻혻혻혻The motor car占퐏 in terror of the Future, and the Past;
혻혻혻혻The motor car is worn out and has passed Sin占퐏 boundary by,
혻혻혻혻And is bound for Caesar占퐏 Column where to pile its riders high.
혻혻혻혻It占퐏 bound for Caesar占퐏 Column
혻혻혻혻And marked for Caesar占퐏 Column,
혻혻혻혻And doomed for Caesar占퐏 Column
혻혻혻혻To pile its riders high.

혻혻혻혻The motor car is brainless, and scornful of all tears,
혻혻혻혻Its dust is in our faces, its giggle in our ears,
혻혻혻혻Its harsh laugh is the last laugh of the last lost soul alone,
혻혻혻혻占폯is nearing Caesar占퐏 Column to set self-damned in stone.
혻혻혻혻Change here for Caesar占퐏 Column!
혻혻혻혻All out for Caesar占퐏 Column!
혻혻혻혻Past Hope, and Caesar占퐏 Column
혻혻혻혻To lodge self-damned in stone.

혻혻혻혻I don占퐐 know how 占퐐will happen, or when 占퐐will come to pass,
혻혻혻혻But folk shall yet pass sanely by river, tree and grass;
혻혻혻혻By homesteads and farm wagons, they占퐇l ride each pleasant mile,
혻혻혻혻And back from Caesar占퐏 Column where the world went mad awhile.
혻혻혻혻And back from Caesar占퐏 Column
혻혻혻혻With lessons from the Column;
혻혻혻혻Grown sane at Caesar占퐏 Column
혻혻혻혻To save the world awhile.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Henry Lawson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Henry Lawson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
